IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important and understudied problem—fine-grained product identification in illicit online marketplaces—providing a novel, large dataset (93,924 posts, 1,938 annotated) across four real forums (Darkode, Hack Forums, Blackhat, Nulled). Their finding of severe cross-forum degradation and the limited effectiveness of standard semi-supervised/domain-adaptation techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III) highlights a substantive methodological gap that will change how researchers approach domain adaptation for fine-grained IE. By releasing annotated token-level data and demonstrating concrete failure modes, the work opens meaningful new research directions for robust cross-domain extraction in adversarial/heterogeneous forums and is likely to be built upon by both NLP and security communities. | B(4): The paper tackles an important, real-world problem by producing a large dataset (93,924 posts with 1,938 token-level annotated posts) for product extraction in cybercrime marketplaces and is reportedly the first to address marketplace forum IE, which is valuable for both security and NLP communities. Its empirical finding of severe cross-forum degradation and that standard semi-supervised/domain-adaptation techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III) have limited effectiveness highlights a substantive challenge that should change how researchers approach fine-grained domain adaptation. These contributions are likely to open significant new research directions and be built upon, though they stop short of a transformative methodological breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important and understudied problem—fine-grained product identification in illicit online marketplaces—providing a novel, large dataset (93,924 posts, 1,938 annotated) across four real forums (Darkode, Hack Forums, Blackhat, Nulled). Their finding of severe cross-forum degradation and the limited effectiveness of standard semi-supervised/domain-adaptation techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III) highlights a substantive methodological gap that will change how researchers approach domain adaptation for fine-grained IE. By releasing annotated token-level data and demonstrating concrete failure modes, the work opens meaningful new research directions for robust cross-domain extraction in adversarial/heterogeneous forums and is likely to be built upon by both NLP and security communities.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important, real-world problem by producing a large dataset (93,924 posts with 1,938 token-level annotated posts) for product extraction in cybercrime marketplaces and is reportedly the first to address marketplace forum IE, which is valuable for both security and NLP communities. Its empirical finding of severe cross-forum degradation and that standard semi-supervised/domain-adaptation techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III) have limited effectiveness highlights a substantive challenge that should change how researchers approach fine-grained domain adaptation. These contributions are likely to open significant new research directions and be built upon, though they stop short of a transformative methodological breakthrough.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a clearly new task/dataset—token-level identification of products in online cybercrime marketplaces with annotations across four distinct forums—and claims to be the first information-extraction treatment of this specific domain, which is a meaningful and novel contribution. While the modeling techniques (CRF, latent SVM, Brown clusters, gazettee rs, Daumé III) are standard, the paper provides novel empirical findings about severe cross-forum degradation and the limited effectiveness of common semi-supervised/domain-adaptation methods on this fine-grained problem, distinguishing it from prior work. These aspects would be of clear interest to experts, even though the algorithmic methods are not themselves new. | B(4): The paper presents a clearly new application and dataset — a token-level product-identification task with annotations across four cybercrime marketplaces (Darkode, Hack Forums, Blackhat, Nulled) and claims to be the first IE work on such marketplace forum data — which is a notable novel contribution. Its empirical finding of severe cross-forum degradation and the demonstrated limits of standard semi-supervised/domain-adaptation techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III) provides new perspective for the community. However, the modeling techniques used (CRF, latent SVM, standard adaptation features) are largely existing methods rather than new algorithms, so the contribution is more novel in task/dataset and empirical analysis than in methodological innovation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a clearly new task/dataset—token-level identification of products in online cybercrime marketplaces with annotations across four distinct forums—and claims to be the first information-extraction treatment of this specific domain, which is a meaningful and novel contribution. While the modeling techniques (CRF, latent SVM, Brown clusters, gazettee rs, Daumé III) are standard, the paper provides novel empirical findings about severe cross-forum degradation and the limited effectiveness of common semi-supervised/domain-adaptation methods on this fine-grained problem, distinguishing it from prior work. These aspects would be of clear interest to experts, even though the algorithmic methods are not themselves new.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a clearly new application and dataset — a token-level product-identification task with annotations across four cybercrime marketplaces (Darkode, Hack Forums, Blackhat, Nulled) and claims to be the first IE work on such marketplace forum data — which is a notable novel contribution. Its empirical finding of severe cross-forum degradation and the demonstrated limits of standard semi-supervised/domain-adaptation techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III) provides new perspective for the community. However, the modeling techniques used (CRF, latent SVM, standard adaptation features) are largely existing methods rather than new algorithms, so the contribution is more novel in task/dataset and empirical analysis than in methodological innovation.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are strong: evaluations across four distinct forums, within- and cross-forum settings, multiple baselines (dictionary, CRF, post-level latent SVM) and domain-adaptation/semi-supervised techniques (+Brown, +Gaz, Daume III) with NP- and token-level metrics support the main claims. The paper includes targeted analyses (feature additions, OOV product impact, NP-length parser mitigation) that serve as useful ablations/sensitivity checks, and related work coverage is reported as adequate. Limitations and negative results are explicitly discussed and hyperparameters are reported, but the summary does not indicate code release or exploration of newer adaptation/neural methods, which are minor gaps for full reproducibility and completeness. | B(4): The paper runs comprehensive within- and cross-forum experiments across four real marketplaces, compares multiple baselines (dictionary, post-level latent SVM, CRF) and several adaptation techniques (+Brown, +gazetteers, Daumé III), and analyzes degradation sources (OOV rates, parser/NLP errors) with reported hyperparameters and inter-annotator agreement. Ablative comparisons are present and negative results/limitations are explicitly discussed. Minor gaps remain (e.g., no mention of released code and exploration of additional or more modern domain-adaptation methods, and the annotated set is modest in size), so while thorough, the empirical coverage has some limitations.

The paper includes targeted analyses (feature additions, OOV product impact, NP-length parser mitigation) that serve as useful ablations/sensitivity checks, and related work coverage is reported as adequate. MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against a strong set of appropriate baselines (dictionary baseline, CRF, a post-level latent SVM) and standard domain-adaptation/semi-supervised techniques (+Brown clusters, gazetteeers, Daume III). Comparisons appear fair — they use the same annotated multi-forum data and consistent NP- and token-level F1 / recall / post-level metrics — and the authors explicitly report when standard prior methods provide limited or comparable gains. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the paper analyzes differences between feature/method variants across within- and cross-forum settings. | B(5): The paper evaluates a broad set of appropriate baselines (dictionary baseline, post-level latent SVM, CRF) and standard domain-adaptation/semi-supervised techniques (+Brown clusters, +gazetteers, Daume III). Comparisons are presented on the same annotated forum datasets with consistent NP/token F1 and post-level metrics, and hyperparameters are reported. The authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods offer limited improvements and analyze failure modes (cross-forum degradation, OOV products, NP projection errors), and related work coverage is described as adequate, supporting a comprehensive and fair comparison.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a strong set of appropriate baselines (dictionary baseline, CRF, a post-level latent SVM) and standard domain-adaptation/semi-supervised techniques (+Brown clusters, gazetteeers, Daume III). APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it formulates a token-level IE task and fine-grained domain adaptation challenge on cybercrime marketplaces and releases an annotated dataset (1,938 posts across Darkode, Hack Forums, Blackhat, Nulled). The methodology and evaluation use standard community tools (CRF, latent SVM/post-level system, Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III) and report NP-/token-level F1 and post-level accuracy, making the work directly relevant to researchers in information extraction and domain adaptation. The finding of severe cross-forum degradation and limited gains from common adaptation techniques is a substantive empirical contribution that will interest the venue’s audience. | B(5): This paper tackles a core NLP/ML problem—fine-grained information extraction and domain adaptation—applied to a concrete, novel dataset (93,924 posts with 1,938 token-level annotations across four forums), which is directly within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The methodology and evaluation use standard, expected tools and metrics (CRF and latent SVM systems, Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III feature adaptation, NP/token-level F1, recall), and the paper explicitly studies cross-domain degradation and semi-/domain-adaptation baselines. The work is well positioned for the community (claims to be the first IE treatment of marketplace forums, exposes a difficult real-world adaptation problem and releases annotated data), making it a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it formulates a token-level IE task and fine-grained domain adaptation challenge on cybercrime marketplaces and releases an annotated dataset (1,938 posts across Darkode, Hack Forums, Blackhat, Nulled). The methodology and evaluation use standard community tools (CRF, latent SVM/post-level system, Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III) and report NP-/token-level F1 and post-level accuracy, making the work directly relevant to researchers in information extraction and domain adaptation. The finding of severe cross-forum degradation and limited gains from common adaptation techniques is a substantive empirical contribution that will interest the venue’s audience.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and the main contributions (token-level product identification task, four annotated forums, and cross-forum adaptation findings) are stated clearly early and followed by a coherent methodology and experiments section. Notation and evaluation are presented consistently (NP- and token-level F1, recall, post-level accuracy, Fleiss' Kappa) and hyperparameters are reported, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples appear informative given the detailed dataset and metric reporting, and related work coverage is adequate. Minor presentation issues remain around the complexity of the token-to-NP projection and parser-error discussion, which could be clarified further for non-specialist readers. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from task definition to annotation methodology to experiments and conclusions, and the main contributions (token-level product identification task, the four-forum annotated dataset, and the cross-forum degradation finding) are stated early. Notation for token- vs NP-level annotations and the model descriptions (CRF, latent SVM) is consistent and hyperparameters and evaluation metrics are clearly reported. Figures, tables, and examples appear effective and informative given the detailed NP/token F1 and post-level analyses. Writing quality is good overall, with only minor presentation points (e.g., parser-projection limitations and the NP-length heuristic) that could be slightly clearer.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are well supported: the paper provides a substantial multi-forum annotated dataset (93,924 posts, 1,938 annotated) and experiments showing severe cross-forum degradation, evaluated with appropriate metrics (token/NP F1, recall, post accuracy) and multiple baselines (dictionary, CRF, latent SVM) plus domain-adaptation techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III). There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the experimental methodology is generally sound (hyperparameters reported, relevant baselines and adaptations tested). Minor concerns are the lack of reported statistical significance testing and potential noise introduced by parser-based NP projection (noted by the authors), but these do not substantially undermine the paper’s main technical conclusions. | B(4): The main claims (token-level product annotation dataset across four forums, severe cross-forum degradation, and limited gains from standard domain-adaptation techniques) are supported by experiments comparing CRF and post-level systems with baselines (Dict, +Brown, +Gaz, Daume III) and NP-/token-level F1 metrics on 1,938 annotated posts drawn from 93,924 posts. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the experimental setup uses reasonable baselines and feature ablations; however, the summary does not report statistical significance testing and the token-to-NP projection depends on a parser (which the authors mitigate but which could introduce errors). Overall the methodology appears sound and conclusions are justified, with only minor concerns about significance reporting and parser-projection noise.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are well supported: the paper provides a substantial multi-forum annotated dataset (93,924 posts, 1,938 annotated) and experiments showing severe cross-forum degradation, evaluated with appropriate metrics (token/NP F1, recall, post accuracy) and multiple baselines (dictionary, CRF, latent SVM) plus domain-adaptation techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III). There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the experimental methodology is generally sound (hyperparameters reported, relevant baselines and adaptations tested). Minor concerns are the lack of reported statistical significance testing and potential noise introduced by parser-based NP projection (noted by the authors), but these do not substantially undermine the paper’s main technical conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims (token-level product annotation dataset across four forums, severe cross-forum degradation, and limited gains from standard domain-adaptation techniques) are supported by experiments comparing CRF and post-level systems with baselines (Dict, +Brown, +Gaz, Daume III) and NP-/token-level F1 metrics on 1,938 annotated posts drawn from 93,924 posts. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the experimental setup uses reasonable baselines and feature ablations; however, the summary does not report statistical significance testing and the token-to-NP projection depends on a parser (which the authors mitigate but which could introduce errors). Overall the methodology appears sound and conclusions are justified, with only minor concerns about significance reporting and parser-projection noise.

main_claims: Formulated a token-level task to identify products in online cybercrime marketplaces and annotated datasets from four forums (Darkode, Hack Forums, Blackhat, Nulled).
Supervised models trained on one forum show substantially decreased accuracy when applied to other forums (severe cross-forum degradation).
Standard semi-supervised and domain adaptation techniques (e.g., Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III features) have limited effectiveness on this fine-grained domain adaptation problem.
Released a dataset of 93,924 posts from 4 forums with annotations for 1,938 posts, and claim to be the first work to tackle marketplace forum data from an information extraction perspective.
_summary.methodology: Annotate product mentions at the token level in posts from four cybercrime forums, convert token annotations to NP-level via an automatic parser when needed, and train sequence/post-level models (CRF and a latent SVM post-level system) using features including Brown clusters and gazetteers to evaluate within- and cross-forum performance and domain adaptation methods.
limitations: Performance is severely degraded in cross-forum (out-of-domain) settings.
Standard semi-supervised and domain adaptation techniques provide limited improvements on this data.
Token-level annotation standard is complex and time-consuming to produce for new domains.
Projection from token-level to noun-phrase level can suffer from parser errors (they limit NP length to <=7 to mitigate).
High out-of-vocabulary (OOV) product rates reduce performance (lower recall on novel products).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - information extraction and domain adaptation for cybercrime marketplace forums
